checks.a_analysis: The HTML document faithfully replicates the visual organization of the source PDF. The sequence of information—from the title page and table of contents through the introductory sections, core environment descriptions, goals, and appendices—is maintained. The visual hierarchy of headings and the flow of information match the original intent, correctly utilizing a single-column layout for narrative text and structured tables for data blocks as seen in the source.

All tables present in the source PDF, including the complex Study Group table (p. 8), the highly detailed financial revenue/expenditure spreadsheets (p. 31-32), the Goal/Objective matrices (p. 34-43), and the various appendices, are correctly rendered as structured HTML tables. Row and column relationships are preserved, and headers are visually distinct from body cells, ensuring the data is readable and understandable for a sighted user.

All meaningful images from the source PDF have been successfully migrated to the HTML. This includes the North Carolina flag and the 'RSS' logo from the title page (p. 1) and the complex 'Figure 1 – Simplified NENA i3 NG911 Conceptual System Diagram' (p. 24). The images are properly displayed without broken links or missing elements.

A thorough visual scan of the HTML output shows no instances of duplicated body content. Each paragraph, table, and figure from the source PDF appears exactly once in the main body of the HTML document. Section headings appearing in the navigation menu/TOC and again as body landmarks is an intentional accessibility feature and not a content duplication defect.
